skill.name: research-grants
skill.description: Draft and review research grant proposal materials for NSF, NIH, DOE, and DARPA, including aims, summaries, budgets, review criteria, and agency-specific compliance guidance.
skill.summary: Draft agency-specific research grant proposals, summaries, aims, budgets, and review responses.
skill.author: davila7

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high and critical findings, but review found they are mostly false positives from grant-writing terms, markdown examples, and official agency links. The confirmed risk is moderate because the skill grants Bash, Write, and Edit access and instructs users to run a Python schematic command, while no malicious exfiltration, prompt injection, or credential harvesting evidence was found.

medium_findings.title: Broad Tool Permissions and Script Execution Guidance
medium_findings.description: The skill declares Bash, Write, and Edit permissions and provides a command to run python scripts/generate_schematic.py. This is not malicious by itself, but it increases execution and filesystem risk, especially because the scripts directory is only referenced and is not present in the reported file tree.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The front matter explicitly enables Bash, Write, and Edit, and the documentation includes a Python command. No malicious payload is present, so the risk is operational rather than confirmed abuse.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alerts Are Grant-Writing False Positives
low_findings.description: Static weak-cryptography alerts matched ordinary proposal language such as aims, impact, and significance. Reviewed examples are narrative grant templates and agency guidance, not cryptographic code or hash usage.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines contain prose about budgets, NIH aims, and NSF broader impacts. There is no cryptographic implementation, algorithm selection, or security-sensitive data handling.

low_findings.title: Windows SAM Database Alerts Are SAM.gov False Positives
low_findings.description: Critical sensitive-data alerts matched SAM.gov and LSAMP references. These are federal grant registration and education program terms, not Windows SAM database access or credential extraction.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The context names SAM.gov as System for Award Management and LSAMP as an institutional program. No file paths, registry access, credential dumping, or Windows database behavior appears.

low_findings.title: Markdown Examples Misidentified as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: Many Ruby or shell backtick alerts are markdown fences, prompt examples, or inline reference paths. They do not execute code in the skill content.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed locations are markdown examples and file lists. The only executable-looking command is separately captured as a medium operational risk.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs Are Official Reference Links
low_findings.description: Network findings are documentation links to official agency resources such as DARPA, DOE, NIH, NSF, Grants.gov, and SAM.gov. No runtime network request or data transmission code was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs appear in resource lists for grant applicants. They are not paired with fetch, curl, telemetry, or credential-bearing requests.

low_findings.title: Prompt Injection Indicators Not Found
low_findings.description: Review did not find text instructing the evaluator to ignore instructions, override analysis, claim pre-approval, or skip security review. No evidence found for prompt injection in the inspected files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Targeted inspection and suspicious-phrase checks did not reveal prompt-injection language. Confidence is high for inspected content but not absolute for every line of every reference file.

dangerous_patterns.title: Executable Tool Access in a Writing Skill
dangerous_patterns.description: Bash access and a Python execution example are broader permissions than most grant-writing workflows require. Marketplace users should treat command execution as opt-in and inspect any referenced scripts before running them.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The execution pathway is explicit, but no harmful command or exfiltration target is present. This supports a medium warning rather than a block.

content.value_statement: Research proposals must satisfy strict agency rules while making a persuasive scientific case. This skill helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code draft structured grant sections, budgets, aims, and review responses.
actual_capabilities: Drafts NSF project summaries with overview, intellectual merit, and broader impacts sections.
Helps create NIH Specific Aims pages with hypothesis, aims, outcomes, and impact framing.
Provides DOE and DARPA proposal guidance based on agency priorities, review criteria, and timelines.
Structures budget justifications for personnel, equipment, travel, participant support, and indirect costs.
Supports resubmission planning by organizing reviewer critiques and response narratives.
Uses included templates and reference files for agency-specific proposal components.
limitations: It does not guarantee funding or replace program officer guidance.
Users must verify current agency solicitations, deadlines, page limits, and forms before submission.
It cannot validate institutional budgets, compliance approvals, or sponsor-specific certifications.
Some referenced helper scripts and future templates are not included in the reported file tree.

use_cases.title: Prepare a First Federal Proposal
use_cases.description: Map a research idea into the required NSF, NIH, DOE, or DARPA structure before drafting full sections.

use_cases.title: Strengthen a Resubmission
use_cases.description: Turn reviewer feedback into a focused revision plan, response narrative, and stronger proposal positioning.

use_cases.title: Support Grant Development Services
use_cases.description: Create checklists, budget justification drafts, and agency-specific review notes for faculty applicants.

prompt_templates.title: Choose the Right Agency
prompt_templates.prompt: Compare NSF, NIH, DOE, and DARPA for my project on [topic]. Recommend the best fit and explain the proposal sections I should prepare first.

prompt_templates.title: Draft a Project Summary
prompt_templates.prompt: Draft an NSF project summary for [project]. Include Overview, Intellectual Merit, and Broader Impacts. Keep the language clear for mixed reviewers.

prompt_templates.title: Build NIH Specific Aims
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me draft a one-page NIH Specific Aims page for [disease or research area]. Include the gap, central hypothesis, three aims, expected outcomes, and impact.

prompt_templates.title: Audit a Full Proposal Package
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my draft grant package against [agency and mechanism] expectations. Identify compliance gaps, weak review criteria, budget issues, and revision priorities.

output_examples.input: Help me outline an NSF proposal for AI methods in climate model evaluation.
output_examples.output: A proposal outline with a one-paragraph overview, intellectual merit themes, broader impacts activities, work packages, milestones, and a review-readiness checklist.

output_examples.input: Review my NIH Specific Aims draft for clarity and feasibility.
output: A concise critique of significance, innovation, approach, and aim independence.
Suggested revisions for the opening gap statement and each aim.
A list of risks, alternatives, and missing preliminary evidence.

output_examples.input: Create a budget justification for a three-year DOE project.
output_examples.output: A structured justification covering personnel effort, equipment need, travel purpose, materials, participant costs, subawards, and agency-aligned cost rationale.

best_practices: Start with the agency solicitation and map every draft section to explicit review criteria.
Provide project details, team roles, budget assumptions, and page limits before asking for a final draft.
Use the output as a review draft and verify all compliance items with institutional grant staff.
anti_patterns: Do not submit generated proposal text without checking current agency rules and solicitation language.
Do not use generic broader impacts, significance, or innovation claims without measurable activities and evidence.
Do not run referenced scripts or commands until you inspect the files and confirm they exist locally.

faq.question: Which agencies does this skill cover?
faq.answer: It covers NSF, NIH, DOE, and DARPA proposal strategy, structure, review criteria, and common supporting documents.

faq.question: Can it write a complete grant proposal?
faq.answer: It can draft sections and organize a proposal package, but the research team must provide project details and verify compliance.

faq.question: Does it include templates?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes templates for NSF project summaries, NIH Specific Aims pages, and budget justifications.

faq.question: Can it help with budget justifications?
faq.answer: Yes. It can structure budget narratives for common cost categories and connect costs to project work.

faq.question: Is it safe to use with confidential proposal ideas?
faq.answer: Use normal confidentiality controls for sensitive proposal details. The skill content itself does not show exfiltration behavior.

faq.question: What should users verify before submission?
faq.answer: Users should verify deadlines, page limits, forms, sponsor instructions, institutional approvals, and budget rates.
